question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
We performed a systematic review of the literature to evaluate the frequency and epidemiology of acute disseminated candidiasis
with skin lesions in neutropenic patients, taking into consideration
possible changes caused by different prophylactic strategies.

question: Were eligibility criteria unambiguous?
answers:
To evaluate the epidemiology of acute disseminated candidiasis with skin lesions in neutropenic patients, taking into consideration changes caused by different prophylactic strategies.
We selected all types of studies that included haematologic patients with neutropenia and candidaemia or acute disseminated candidiasis and provided a description of the clinical presentation, frequency and type of skin lesion, documentation of skin involvement by Candida and species causing infection.

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers: Reports with limited information (including abstracts), duplicate records, review papers or articles reporting skin lesions associated with infections caused by other pathogens were excluded.

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers:
A computerized search of the Medline database via PubMed was
performed for articles published from 1963 through September 2016 in the English language.

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers: A computerized search of the Medline database via PubMed was performed for articles published from 1963 through September 2016

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers:
hand searching was performed in the reference lists
of all identified studies to find relevant articles.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers: using the following keywords: 'candidiasis,' 'neutropenia or leukaemia' and 'skin or cutaneous.'

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers:
A computerized search of the Medline database via PubMed was
performed for articles published from 1963 through September 2016 in the English language.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers: nan

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers: The data were extracted independently by one author (MG) and reviewed by the other (MN).

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers:
The following data were extracted from the articles: underlying disease and its treatment, type of treatment (induction, consolidation, therapy of relapse, other), clinical presentation, frequency, pattern and site of skin lesions, species causing invasive candidiasis, information about culture and histopathology of the skin, antifungal prophylaxis, primary treatment and outcome. Special attention was devoted to the type of skin lesion, species causing acute disseminated candidiasis and antifungal prophylaxis.We also looked at missing information in each study and the potential limitations caused by the lack of information.

A summary of the characteristics of the cases, including the number of cases with missing information in each variable, is shown in Supplementary Table S2

Five studies reported the frequency of skin lesions. The first was
published in 1974 and evaluated 77 cases of disseminated candidiasis
in a 5-year period at a cancer hospital. Skin lesions were
present in ten cases (13%) . Another study evaluated 89
consecutive haematologic patients with neutropenia and found 18
cases of acute disseminated candidiasis, eight of which (44.4%)
presented with skin lesions . A third study reported 23 cases of
candidaemia with skin lesions among 200 episodes of candidaemia
at a cancer centre (11.5%) . The fourth study evaluated 28 patients
with acute leukaemia who developed candidaemia. Eleven
(39.3%) had skin lesions . The fifth study evaluated 53 consecutive
patients with candidaemia and positive cultures from other
sites. Nineteen (35.8%) presented with skin lesions .

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Our study has some important limitations. Firstly, it includes mostly case reports; secondly, the analysis is derived mostly from
older studies, which are not representative of current standards of
antifungal prophylaxis in haematologic patients and treatment of
acute disseminated candidiasis; and thirdly, some articles did not
include important information such as antifungal prophylaxis, details
of culture and histopathology of skin lesions.

question: Was the relevance of identified studies to the review's research question appropriately considered?
answers:
A total of 100 cases of acute disseminated candidiasis with skin
lesions were identified. A summary of the characteristics of the
cases, including the number of cases with missing information in
each variable, is shown in Supplementary Table S2. The most
frequent underlying disease was acute leukaemia, comprising 86%
of cases (54 acute myeloid leukaemia and 32 acute lymphoid
leukaemia). Other underlying conditions were chronic myeloid
leukaemia in blastic phase (n  1/4 8), aplastic anaemia (n  1/4 3), and
myelodysplastic syndrome, non-Hodgkin lymphoma and posttransplantation
lymphoproliferative disease (n  1/4 1 patient each).
The majority of cases occurred during induction chemotherapy for
recently diagnosed (31%) or relapsed (25%) patients. Of note, only
three cases occurred during consolidation chemotherapy. The information
was not available in 32 cases.
The most frequent Candida species was C. tropicalis (68%), followed
by C. krusei (15%) and C. albicans (10%). For two patients,
histopathologic diagnosis was attempted, but no organism was
recovered. The remaining five cases were caused by
C. guilliermondii (two cases), and C. ciferrii, C. glabrata and C. albicans
plus C. tropicalis (one case each).
The most frequent pattern of the skin lesions reported was
erythematous or purpuric maculopapular lesions (44%), followed
by purpuric or erythematous papular lesions, sometimes with a
pale centre (22%), nodules (17%) and subcutaneous abscess (6%).
Some patients presented with a mixture of different types of lesions,
such as papules and nodules (7%). The site of lesion was reported
in 72 cases: in 44 the lesions were located in the extremities,
and in 28 they were disseminated.
Skin biopsy results with description of histopathology were
available in 51 cases. In 35 cases (68.6%), fungal elements (pseudohyphae,
hyphae and yeasts) were present in the dermis as well as
within blood vessels. In four cases, the pattern was that of an abscess.
Granulomas, necrosis within the wall of a blood vessel, leukocytoclastic
vasculitis, and perivascular infiltrates and thrombi in
small vascular structures were also described. Positive cultures of
skin biopsy samples were reported in 41 (71.9%) of 57 cases. The
most frequent species recovered in cultures of skin biopsy samples
was C. tropicalis (35 cases), followed by C. krusei (five cases).
Involvement of other organs was reported in 44 patients, with
multiple organs such as spleen, liver, kidneys and lungs involved in
the majority of cases. Involvement of more than two organs was
reported in 26 of the 44 cases.
Positive blood cultures were reported in 67 of the 68 cases
caused by C. tropicalis. The most frequent type of skin lesion in such
cases was diffuse maculopapular lesions, reported in 39 (57.3%) of
the 68 cases. The most frequent clinical presentations among the 15
cases caused by C. krusei were nodular (seven cases) or papular
(seven cases) lesions.
Information about antifungal prophylaxis was not available in
the majority of cases (55%). Prophylaxis with fluconazole was reported
in six cases. The species involved in these cases were
C. krusei in five and C. ciferrii in one.

answers: Yes

candidiasis and provided a description of the clinical presentation,

question: Did the review authors provide a list of excluded studies and justify the exclusions?
answers:
Among the 183 articles, 43 met the eligibility
criteria. Of those, ten were excluded for the following reasons: lack
of detailed clinical description (n  1/4 4), cases with skin lesions from
other causes (n  1/4 2), cases without cutaneous involvement (n  1/4 2),
case reports in nonneutropenic patients (n  1/4 1) and review papers
(n =1)
